metadata.visit_title: GI clinic — pre-ERCP consult for suspected PSC dominant stricture progression, brushings requested to exclude cholangiocarcinoma

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: Condition cholangiocarcinoma present as provisional/differential (verificationStatus = provisional); MRCP report reads 'cannot exclude malignancy'
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: referring hepatologist and patient both frame it as a possibility only; no tissue diagnosis; ERCP brushings are being done precisely to determine whether cancer is present
planted_discrepancies.note: TRAP: cholangiocarcinoma is SUSPECTED, not diagnosed. Must remain a suspicion/differential and must NOT be elevated to a confirmed cancer diagnosis. The suspicion legitimately drives urgency/expedite.

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: MedicationRequest list shows ursodiol only; mesalamine not on the active med list despite PMH documenting UC on mesalamine
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient takes mesalamine 1.2 g daily every day for his ulcerative colitis and has not missed it
planted_discrepancies.note: narrative-vs-list conflict; mesalamine is an active maintenance drug missing from the structured med list

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: no anticoagulant or antiplatelet on file
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient describes fish oil as his 'blood thinner'; it is an OTC omega-3 supplement, not a true anticoagulant/antiplatelet
planted_discrepancies.note: peri-procedure TRAP: 'blood thinner' = fish oil. No prescription anticoagulation to hold; do not mislabel patient as anticoagulated. Fish oil noted for peri-ERCP planning only.

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: worsening cholestatic labs consistent with progressing dominant stricture; corroborates need for ERCP
rising ALP, new dominant stricture on MRCP, and inability to exclude malignancy make expedited ERCP appropriate
planted_discrepancies.note: urgency label is accurate, not mislabeled — genuine expedite

prior_notes.text: Established PSC, diagnosed 2016 on MRCP/ERCP with characteristic beaded intra- and extrahepatic ducts; associated ulcerative colitis. On ursodiol 300 mg TID. Interval labs stable at that time: ALP 320 U/L, total bilirubin 1.1 mg/dL, AST 41, ALT 38. No jaundice, pruritus, cholangitis, or weight loss. Continue ursodiol; annual MRCP surveillance for cholangiocarcinoma given PSC. CA 19-9 to trend. Return 6 months or sooner for rising cholestatic labs, jaundice, or fever.

Ulcerative colitis, left-sided, quiescent. Maintained on mesalamine 1.2 g daily. No rectal bleeding, urgency, or nocturnal stools; normal stool frequency. Last surveillance colonoscopy with random biopsies negative for dysplasia. Concurrent PSC followed by hepatology. Continue mesalamine; continue PSC-IBD dysplasia surveillance colonoscopy interval of 1 year.

code.text: ERCP with cholangiography, dominant stricture assessment, and biliary brushing cytology (planned)

DiagnosticReport.conclusion: Known PSC with multifocal intra- and extrahepatic strictures. New dominant stricture of the common hepatic duct with upstream ductal dilatation, increased from prior. Associated subtle mural enhancement. Findings are worrisome for progression; a superimposed cholangiocarcinoma CANNOT be excluded. Tissue sampling (ERCP with brushings) recommended.
PSC with stable multifocal strictures. No dominant stricture. No mass. No definite features of malignancy.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Andrade. Thanks for coming in on short notice. Your hepatologist asked us to look at your bile ducts with an ERCP, and I want to walk through why and make sure the plan fits with what you're feeling.
MA: Before we start, let me get your vitals — height 5 foot 9, weight 176 pounds. Temp is 36.7, heart rate 72, respirations 14, blood pressure 122 over 76, and oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Morning. Yeah, Dr. Okafor called me herself. She said the MRI showed a new narrowing and she wants a closer look — and to take a brush sample to be safe.
DR: That's right. Your PSC has been stable for years, but this scan shows a new dominant stricture that wasn't there last summer, and your alkaline phosphatase has climbed — it was around 320 in January, it's 480 now, and your bilirubin is up a touch to 1.9.
PT: She mentioned the numbers. She was careful to say it's not cancer — but that they can't rule cancer out on the picture alone. That's the part that scares me.
DR: I want to be just as careful with the words. Right now cholangiocarcinoma is a possibility we have to exclude, not a diagnosis. The whole point of the brushings is to answer that question. So we're doing this promptly, but nobody is telling you that you have cancer today.
PT: Okay. That helps, honestly.
DR: Let me check your medicines. I have ursodiol three times a day.
PT: Yes, every day, no misses.
DR: What about for your colitis?
PT: The mesalamine — the 1.2 gram once a day. I take that too, every morning. My gut's been quiet, no bleeding, no rushing to the bathroom.
DR: Good, and I want that mesalamine on your list — it only had the ursodiol. Any blood thinners?
PT: Just fish oil. Somebody told me that thins the blood, so I count it.
DR: That's helpful, but fish oil isn't a true blood thinner — you're not on warfarin, Eliquis, aspirin, anything like that?
PT: No, nothing like that. Just the fish oil capsules.
DR: Perfect, that simplifies the procedure. Any allergies?
PT: None that I know of.
DR: We'll get the ERCP with brushings scheduled quickly, and the team will send prep and fasting instructions.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Andrade is a 55-year-old man with primary sclerosing cholangitis (diagnosed 2016) and associated quiescent ulcerative colitis, referred by hepatology for pre-procedure evaluation prior to ERCP. Surveillance MRCP (2026-07-02) shows a NEW dominant stricture of the common hepatic duct, increased from the prior stable 2025 study, with upstream dilatation; the radiologist states a superimposed cholangiocarcinoma cannot be excluded and recommends tissue sampling. The patient is aware and appropriately frames malignancy as a possibility being excluded, not an established diagnosis. He reports no jaundice, pruritus, fever/rigors, or weight loss. UC is quiescent — no rectal bleeding, urgency, or nocturnal stools. He confirms ursodiol 300 mg TID and mesalamine 1.2 g daily with good adherence. He reports taking OTC fish oil, which he refers to as a 'blood thinner'; he denies any prescription anticoagulant or antiplatelet (no warfarin, DOAC, aspirin, or clopidogrel). No known drug allergies.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.7 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 122/76, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 175 cm (5'9"), Wt 80 kg (176 lb), BMI 26.1. Cholestatic labs (2026-07-10): ALP 480 U/L (up from 320 in Jan 2026), total bilirubin 1.9 mg/dL, GGT 410 U/L, ALT 62 U/L, CA 19-9 68 U/mL. MRCP 2026-07-02: new dominant common hepatic duct stricture with upstream dilatation; malignancy cannot be excluded.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- PSC with NEW dominant biliary stricture and rising cholestatic labs (ALP 320 -> 480, bili 1.9) — progression; expedited ERCP with cholangiography and biliary brushing cytology indicated.
- Suspected cholangiocarcinoma — SUSPICION ONLY, not tissue-confirmed. MRCP cannot exclude malignancy; ERCP brushings (+/- FISH) are being performed to establish or exclude the diagnosis. Do not document as a confirmed cancer. Elevated CA 19-9 is nonspecific in the setting of cholestasis/stricture.
- Ulcerative colitis — quiescent, continue mesalamine 1.2 g daily. Add mesalamine to the active medication list (was missing; ursodiol only).
- Continue ursodiol 300 mg TID.
- Peri-procedure: no prescription anticoagulant/antiplatelet — patient's 'blood thinner' is OTC fish oil, not a true anticoagulant; no bridging or reversal required. Advise holding fish oil per pre-procedure instructions. No known drug allergies.
- Coordinate ERCP scheduling on an expedited basis; provide bowel/fasting prep and hold instructions; return precautions for jaundice, fever/chills, or right upper quadrant pain (cholangitis).
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Your liver-duct condition (PSC) and a new narrowed area seen on your MRI scan
• Rising liver-duct blood tests (alkaline phosphatase and bilirubin)
• A planned procedure called ERCP, where we take a small brush sample of the narrowed duct
• Why we are doing this soon

Important
• The scan cannot tell us for certain what is causing the narrowing. Cancer is one possibility we need to rule out — it is NOT a diagnosis you have been given today. The brush sample is how we find out.

Next steps
• We will schedule your ERCP with brushings promptly; the team will send fasting and preparation instructions and tell you what to stop beforehand.
• Keep taking your ursodiol three times a day and your mesalamine each morning.
• We updated your record to include your mesalamine.
• Your fish oil is not a true blood thinner, but plan to pause it before the procedure per the instructions we send.
• Call right away for yellowing of the eyes or skin, fever or chills, or belly pain.
